sections.section_title: 12.1 How Do We Experience and Interpret Our Surroundings?

content.text: A habitat is a place where an organism lives and can be as small as the bark of a tree or as large as a forest. Habitats provide specific living conditions and resources needed for organisms to survive and thrive. Different habitats have different types of plants, animals, and living conditions because organisms adapt to their specific environments.

Every habitat contains two types of components: (1) Biotic components—the living organisms including plants, animals, and microorganisms. (2) Abiotic components—non-living physical factors such as air, water, soil, sunlight, temperature, and humidity. Together, these components create conditions suitable for specific organisms.

A pond habitat has fish, frogs, aquatic plants, and dragonflies (biotic components) along with water, soil, sunlight, and temperature (abiotic components). The fish need oxygen from water, food from small plants and animals, shelter among plants, and space to grow. Different organisms in the same pond (snake active at night, rodent active during day) experience different conditions, allowing coexistence in harmony.

sections.section_title: 12.2 Who All Live Together in Nature?

content.text: A population is a group of organisms of the same kind living together in a habitat at a given time. For example, all fish of the same species in a pond form a population. Population size can be determined by counting individuals in a defined area and time.

A community comprises different populations sharing the same habitat. The biotic components (plants, animals, and microorganisms) together form the community. These organisms interact and depend on one another for survival. Different species in a community compete for the same resources (food, water, space), use resources differently, and have different roles.

sections.section_title: 12.3 Does Every Organism in a Community Matter?

content.text: Every organism in a community plays a role in the ecosystem, even if the role is indirect. A research study comparing two ponds demonstrated this: Pond A (with fish) had fewer dragonflies, more bees/butterflies, and more flowering plants producing seeds. Pond B (without fish) had more dragonflies, fewer bees/butterflies, and fewer flowering plants producing seeds.

The mechanism: Fish eat dragonfly larvae, reducing dragonflies. Without dragonflies, which eat bees and butterflies, insect pollinators increase. More pollinators mean more plant pollination and seed production. This shows how biotic components (fish, dragonflies, pollinators, plants) and abiotic components (temperature, water, nutrients) interact and affect each other. Removing one organism can have cascading effects throughout the ecosystem.

sections.section_title: 12.4 What Are the Different Types of Interactions Among Organisms and Their Surroundings?

content.text: An ecosystem is formed by the interaction between biotic components (organisms) and abiotic components (non-living factors) in a habitat. Living organisms depend on abiotic components for survival, and abiotic components also depend on biotic components for maintenance.

Three types of interactions occur in ecosystems: (1) Between abiotic and biotic components—influence life processes like nutrition, respiration, and reproduction. (2) Between two abiotic components—influence physical characteristics of the habitat. (3) Among biotic components—influence availability of resources for life processes.

Earthworms live in moist soil (biotic-abiotic interaction); sunlight causes temperature increase (abiotic-abiotic interaction); frogs eat insects (biotic-biotic interaction). Sunlight, carbon dioxide, and water are essential for plant food production. Soil provides nutrients for plant growth. Air provides oxygen for respiration. Water is essential for all organisms. Plants return oxygen to air, roots prevent soil erosion, plants retain soil moisture and cool the atmosphere.

items: Aquatic ecosystems—ponds, rivers, lakes, oceans; organisms adapted to water environments
Terrestrial ecosystems—forests, grasslands, deserts, farmland; organisms adapted to land environments
Human-made ecosystems—fish ponds, farms, parks; created by humans to meet needs; require ongoing management
Overlapping ecosystems—different ecosystem types can interact (river flowing through forest and grassland)

sections.section_title: 12.5 Who Eats Whom? - Food Chains and Food Webs

content.text: Organisms are classified based on their feeding relationships: (1) Producers (Autotrophs)—organisms that make their own food through photosynthesis; all green plants are producers. (2) Consumers (Heterotrophs)—organisms that depend on other organisms for food; cannot produce their own food.

Consumers are further classified: (1) Herbivores—eat only plants (deer, hare, squirrel). (2) Carnivores—eat only animals (leopard, tiger, eagle). (3) Omnivores—eat both plants and animals (crows, foxes, mice). (4) Decomposers—break down dead plants and animals, returning nutrients to soil (bacteria, fungi, mushrooms).

A food chain is a simple sequence showing 'who eats whom' in an ecosystem. It shows how energy flows from one organism to another. Example: Grass → Grasshopper → Frog → Snake → Eagle. Each organism occupies a specific position called a trophic level: (1) First trophic level—Producers (plants). (2) Second level—Primary consumers/Herbivores (grasshoppers, hares). (3) Third level—Secondary consumers (frogs, small carnivores). (4) Fourth level—Tertiary consumers (large carnivores, eagles).

A food web is a network of interconnected food chains in an ecosystem. While a food chain shows a linear feeding relationship, a food web shows that an organism may eat multiple types of food and may be eaten by multiple predators. For example, grass might be eaten by grasshopper, hare, and deer. Each organism can be part of multiple food chains.

A food web demonstrates the complexity of ecological relationships and shows how all organisms in an ecosystem are interconnected. The removal of one organism can affect multiple other species through various feeding pathways.

sections.section_title: 12.6 What Happens to Waste in Nature?

content.text: Decomposition is the process by which organic (dead) matter is broken down into simpler substances. Decomposers are organisms that carry out decomposition. Decomposers include bacteria and fungi that break down complex substances in dead plants and animals into simpler substances. These simpler substances are returned to the soil as nutrients.

Mushrooms grow on dead plants and trees during rainy season. Beetles and flies on elephant dung help break it down. These organisms recycle nutrients back into the environment. Plants grow in soil and many nutrients come from decomposition. In nature, nothing is wasted—everything is recycled.

sections.section_title: 12.7 How Does One Change Lead to Another?

content.text: One small change in an ecosystem can lead to many other changes through cascading effects. When plants die due to pollution, less oxygen is produced in water, leading to drop in fish population. Fewer fish lead to fewer consumers, so insects increase. These insects spread to farmlands, forcing farmers to use pesticides, which further damages the environment.

In the 1980s, large-scale harvesting of Indian bullfrogs for export led to population decline. Since frogs eat insects, reduced frog numbers resulted in increased agricultural pests. Farmers had to use more synthetic pesticides, which harmed the environment and human health. Government banned frog leg export to prevent further ecological damage.

An ecosystem maintains balance when interactions among organisms and their environment keep populations and resources stable. This balance is dynamic, not fixed. Natural changes (weather, seasonal variations) and human-made changes (pollution, habitat destruction) can disrupt this balance.

sections.section_title: 12.8 How Do Interactions Maintain Balance in Ecosystems?

content.text: Competition is the struggle among organisms for common resources like food, water, physical space, and sunlight. Competition helps control population size and keeps the ecosystem balanced. Without competition, one species could multiply excessively, causing ecosystem imbalance.

items: Mutualism: Both organisms benefit. Example: Honeybees and flowers (bees get nectar, flowers get pollinated)
Commensalism: One organism benefits; the other is not affected. Example: Orchids on trees (orchid gets support, tree unaffected)
Parasitism: One organism benefits while the other is harmed. Example: Ticks on dogs (ticks get nutrition, dogs are harmed)

content.text: These interactions are part of the complex web of life in an ecosystem. Competition and symbiotic relationships together maintain the balance and stability of ecosystems.

sections.section_title: 12.9 What Are the Benefits of an Ecosystem?

content.text: Humans depend on ecosystems for numerous benefits: (1) Forests provide fresh air, fertile soil, food, fibres, timber, and medicines. (2) Aquatic ecosystems provide water and food. (3) Ecosystems offer aesthetic and recreational value. (4) Ecosystems regulate climate, cycle nutrients, and purify water and air.

The close connection between nature and humans shows that when we overuse or misuse natural resources, we disturb the balance and harm ourselves. Examples: The Sundarbans mangrove forests protect against storms and floods, absorb carbon dioxide, and release oxygen. Protected areas (national parks, sanctuaries, biosphere reserves) help preserve these ecosystems.

items: Deforestation—cutting trees removes habitat and biodiversity
Pollution—industrial waste, sewage, pesticides harm organisms and water quality
Habitat loss—destroying ecosystems removes homes for organisms
Climate change—alters temperature and rainfall patterns affecting organisms
Invasive species—non-native species outcompete native species
Overexploitation—overfishing, overhunting, overuse of resources depletes populations

content.text: The Sundarbans mangrove forests (world's largest) are threatened by tree-cutting for fuelwood, illegal hunting, overuse of forest resources, and pollution. Wildlife includes endangered species that depend on these forests.

Humans have created artificial ecosystems like fish ponds, farms, and parks to meet their needs. When well-designed, these can reduce pollution, support biodiversity, and provide recreational spaces. Unlike natural ecosystems, these require ongoing human care and management.

Between 1950-1965, India faced a food crisis. The Green Revolution (mid-20th century) used tractors, machines, synthetic fertilizers, and pesticides to increase food production. However, these methods are unsustainable because of overuse of synthetic chemicals, excessive groundwater extraction, and monoculture (growing only one crop).

Problems with unsustainable farming: (1) Overuse of synthetic fertilizers—reduces soil fertility by decreasing helpful microorganisms and organic matter (humus), making soil prone to erosion. (2) Pesticide use—reduces populations of natural predators, ultimately increasing pest populations. (3) Monoculture—reduces crop diversity and affects pollinators crucial for food production. (4) Heavy irrigation and ploughing—disturbs soil organisms like earthworms important for ecological balance. (5) Pest resistance—pests may develop resistance to pesticides, making them difficult to control.

Sustainable farming practices: (1) Organic and natural farming—reduce synthetic fertilizer use; support natural ecosystems. (2) Use traditional methods—apply Vrikshayurveda principles emphasizing soil health through organic manure (Kunapa Jala—liquid fertilizer from fermented plant and animal waste). (3) Protect soil—maintain earthworms and other soil organisms. (4) Support pollinators—maintain biodiversity to support natural pollination. (5) Minimize interference—work with natural processes rather than against them.
Protected areas are parts of land or water set aside to conserve wildlife and their habitats. These help protect entire habitats including endangered animals, birds, and rare plants.

items: Jim Corbett National Park (Uttarakhand)
Manas National Park (Assam)
Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve (Western Ghats)
Chilika Lake (Odisha)
Eaglenest Wildlife Sanctuary (Arunachal Pradesh)
Hemis National Park (Leh)
Keibul Lamjao National Park (Manipur)
Pirotan Island Marine National Park (Gujarat)

content.text: Protected areas play a crucial role in saving nature for future generations by preserving biodiversity and maintaining ecosystem functions.

glossary.definition: A place where an organism lives and can be as small as tree bark or as large as a forest; provides specific living conditions for organisms.
Living organisms in a habitat including plants, animals, and microorganisms.
Non-living physical factors in a habitat including air, water, soil, sunlight, temperature, and humidity.
A group of organisms of the same kind living together in a habitat at a given time.
Different populations sharing the same habitat; the biotic components of a habitat that interact and depend on each other.
The interaction between biotic components (organisms) and abiotic components (non-living factors) in a habitat.
An organism that makes its own food through photosynthesis; all green plants are producers (autotrophs).
An organism that depends on other organisms for food and cannot produce its own food (heterotrophs).
A consumer that eats only plants and plant products (e.g., deer, hare, squirrel).
A consumer that eats only animals (e.g., leopard, tiger, eagle).
A consumer that eats both plants and animals (e.g., crows, foxes, mice).
An organism that breaks down dead plants and animals into simpler substances, returning nutrients to soil (e.g., bacteria, fungi, mushrooms).
A simple sequence showing 'who eats whom' in an ecosystem, showing how energy flows from one organism to another.
A network of interconnected food chains in an ecosystem showing complex feeding relationships.
The position or level that an organism occupies in a food chain (producer, primary consumer, secondary consumer, etc.).
The process of breaking down dead organic matter into simpler substances by decomposers.
The struggle among organisms for common resources like food, water, space, and sunlight.
A symbiotic relationship where both organisms benefit from each other (e.g., bees and flowers).
A symbiotic relationship where one organism benefits while the other is not affected (e.g., orchids on trees).
A symbiotic relationship where one organism benefits while the other is harmed (e.g., ticks on dogs).

summary_points.description: A habitat is a place where organisms live, containing biotic components (living things: plants, animals, microbes) and abiotic components (non-living factors: air, water, soil, sunlight, temperature). The interaction between biotic and abiotic components forms an ecosystem. Ecosystems can be aquatic (ponds, lakes, rivers) or terrestrial (forests, grasslands, farms). Each ecosystem has unique characteristics adapted to specific organisms.

Organisms are organized into hierarchies: Individuals are single organisms. A population is a group of same-kind organisms in a habitat. A community comprises different populations in the same habitat interacting and depending on each other. An ecosystem is the community plus all abiotic factors. Understanding these levels helps explain ecological relationships and interconnections.

Every organism in an ecosystem, even indirectly, affects other organisms and the environment. Fish eating dragonfly larvae reduces dragonflies, which increases pollinators, which increases plant seed production. Removing one organism causes cascading effects throughout the ecosystem. This demonstrates complex interdependence—removing even small organisms disrupts ecosystem balance.

Organisms are classified by feeding relationships: Producers (plants) make their own food. Consumers include herbivores (eat plants), carnivores (eat animals), and omnivores (eat both). Decomposers (bacteria, fungi) break down dead matter and recycle nutrients. Food chains show linear feeding relationships; food webs show complex interconnected relationships. Energy flows from producers through consumers at different trophic levels to decomposers.

Decomposers break down dead organisms and waste, returning nutrients to soil. This process recycles essential nutrients making them available for plants again. In nature, nothing is wasted—all organic matter is recycled. Decomposition is essential for soil health, nutrient availability, and ecosystem functioning. Without decomposers, ecosystems would be clogged with dead matter.

One small change in an ecosystem can have far-reaching consequences through cascade effects. Loss of forest cover leads to drying waterholes, forcing animals into human habitats. Frog overharvesting increased pests, forcing pesticide use which harmed the environment. Ecosystem balance is dynamic and can be disrupted by natural or human-made changes. Understanding these connections helps prevent unintended harm.

Competition for resources (food, water, space, sunlight) controls population size and prevents dominance of one species. Symbiotic relationships include mutualism (both benefit), commensalism (one benefits, other unaffected), and parasitism (one benefits, one harmed). Together, competition and symbiotic relationships maintain ecosystem stability and biodiversity.

Humans depend on ecosystems for numerous benefits: forests provide air, soil, food, timber, medicines; aquatic ecosystems provide water and food; ecosystems offer aesthetic and recreational value; climate regulation and nutrient cycling support all life. When we overuse or misuse resources, we disrupt ecosystem balance and harm ourselves. Protected areas preserve ecosystems and biodiversity for future generations.

Unsustainable farming (synthetic chemicals, monoculture, excessive irrigation) damages soil, reduces biodiversity, and increases pest resistance. Sustainable practices include organic farming, protecting soil organisms, supporting pollinators, and minimizing environmental interference. Traditional practices like Vrikshayurveda emphasize soil health through organic manure. Sustainable farming balances food production with environmental protection, ensuring both human and ecosystem well-being for the future.
